records.ety: [F. composer; com- + poser to place. The sense is that of L. componere, but the origin is different. See Pose, v. t.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Composed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Composing.]

defs: To form by putting together two or more things or parts; to put together; to make up; to fashion.
quotes: Zeal ought to be composed of the highest degrees of all pious affection.

defs: To form the substance of, or part of the substance of; to constitute.
quotes: Their borrowed gold composed The calf in Oreb.
A few useful things . . . compose their intellectual possessions.

defs: To construct by mental labor; to design and execute, or put together, in a manner involving the adaptation of forms of expression to ideas, or to the laws of harmony or proportion; as, to compose a sentence, a sermon, a symphony, or a picture.
quotes: Let me compose Something in verse as well as prose.
The genius that composed such works as the “Standard” and “Last Supper”.

defs: To dispose in proper form; to reduce to order; to put in proper state or condition; to adjust; to regulate.
quotes: In a peaceful grave my corpse compose.
How in safety best we may Compose our present evils.

defs: To free from agitation or disturbance; to tranquilize; to soothe; to calm; to quiet.
quotes: Compose thy mind; Nor frauds are here contrived, nor force designed.

defs: To arrange (types) in a composing stick in order for printing; to set (type).
